title: Danus: Orchestrating Mathematical Reasoning Agents with Fact-Graph Memory
authors: Jihao Liu, Guoxiong Gao, Zeming Sun, Bin Wu, Shurui Liu, Jiedong Jiang
abstract: Recent LLM-based mathematical reasoning agents have begun to tackle research-level problems and, in several cases, have contributed to the resolution of open problems. However, scaling and orchestrating such agents effectively remains challenging, due to the difficulty of coordinating parallel proof search while keeping intermediate claims organized and reliable. In this paper, we propose Danus, an orchestration system for research-level mathematical reasoning centered on a shared fact graph as a global memory-management mechanism. Danus consists of a main agent that performs planning and coordination, multiple worker agents that carry out proof search in parallel, and a stateless verifier that checks proposed mathematical claims before they are admitted into the fact graph. Each verified fact is stored together with its proof and logical dependencies, allowing the system to build long arguments incrementally while keeping the shared proof state organized. The main agent periodically summarizes the evolving proof state, redirects workers across promising directions, and supports interaction with human mathematicians through progress reports. We evaluate Danus through six research-level case studies in algebraic geometry, singularity theory, and combinatorics, illustrating how the fact-graph memory mechanism enables Danus to construct long, detailed mathematical proofs. Our results suggest that fact-graph-based orchestration provides an effective route toward scaling mathematical reasoning agents for long-horizon research problems. Danus is open source at https://github.com/frenzymath/Danus.

infographic.key_insight: Danus coordinates multiple math-reasoning agents around a shared fact graph, turning verified intermediate claims into organized long-horizon proof memory.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The design offers a practical orchestration pattern for scaling agentic theorem-proving systems while preserving traceability, consistency, and human reviewability.
method: Main agent plans, summarizes, and redirects proof search across workers.
Worker agents explore proof directions in parallel.
A stateless verifier checks claims before adding them, with each fact stored alongside proof and dependencies in the fact graph.
infographic.evidence_signal: The paper evaluates Danus on six research-level case studies spanning algebraic geometry, singularity theory, and combinatorics.
infographic.watch_item: The approach depends on reliable verification and dependency management; errors or bottlenecks there could weaken the shared fact graph and limit scalability.
infographic.paper_title: Danus: Orchestrating Mathematical Reasoning Agents with Fact-Graph Memory

title: MechMath Agent Team: LLM Driven Agents for Mathematical Research
authors: Yichuan Cao, Ruichen Qiu, Junqi Liu, Jiaqi Wang, Dakai Guo, Ruyong Feng
abstract: AI reasoning has become a central focus in contemporary artificial intelligence, largely driven by the success of large language models. However, mathematical research, which is characterized by non-linear derivation paths, rigorous logical requirements, and protracted exploration cycles, poses severe challenges for existing reasoning systems. To overcome these limitations, we present the MechMath Agent Team (MMAT), which is a large language model driven agent designed to serve as a co-pilot throughout the full cycle of mathematical research. We design a tripartite Harness Architecture that decouples system responsibilities into Control, Execution, and Augmentation planes, thereby reconciling rigorous logical control with the agility demanded by open-ended research. Building upon this framework, we instantiate three specialized agents: a Knowledge Base Manager, a Natural Language Prover, and a Formal Language Prover, all operating in a closed loop to produce formally certified mathematical proofs. We evaluate MMAT on open problems in Number Theory, Algebraic Complexity Theory, Differential Algebra, Operator Algebra, and Inequalities. Across a two-month deployment, 11 problems have been solved, demonstrating its capacity to act as a co-pilot throughout the entire research cycle. The contributions are threefold: a general decoupled Harness Architecture for multi-agent mathematical reasoning, its concrete instantiation in the MMAT system, and empirical validation on a diverse suite of open problems.

infographic.key_insight: MMAT is a multi-agent LLM system that supports the full mathematical research loop by separating control, execution, and augmentation into a harness architecture.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The decoupled design offers a practical blueprint for building reliable theorem-proving agents that combine rigorous formal verification with flexible exploratory reasoning.
method: Tripartite Harness Architecture with Control, Execution, and Augmentation planes
Three specialized agents: Knowledge Base Manager, Natural Language Prover, Formal Language Prover
Closed-loop workflow from knowledge management to natural-language reasoning to formal proof certification
infographic.evidence_signal: The system was evaluated on open problems across five math areas over a two-month deployment, solving 11 problems.
infographic.watch_item: Results come from a relatively small, domain-specific deployment, so generalization and robustness across broader mathematical workloads remain uncertain.
infographic.paper_title: MechMath Agent Team: LLM Driven Agents for Mathematical Research

title: Physics-Informed Neural Embeddings of PDE Solution Families
authors: Raul Jimenez, Svitlana Mayboroda, Pavlos Protopapas, Leonid Sarieddine, David N. Spergel, Pedro Tarancón-Álvarez
abstract: We introduce a physics-informed framework for learning finite-dimensional embeddings of solution families of partial differential equations. The method uses a multihead Physics-Informed Neural Network in which a shared body learns a latent manifold representing the solution space, while linear heads reconstruct individual solutions associated with different initial conditions. A head-orthogonalization penalty removes degeneracies in the latent representation and stabilizes the principal-component spectrum across training realizations. Because the initial condition is built into the network output by construction, these principal components measure the additional variability the network learns on top of the initial profile, not the full solution itself. We apply the method to the one-dimensional viscous Burgers equation, with the heat and wave equations as robustness checks. For a latent dimension $n_b=20$, the learned manifolds exhibit pronounced effective dimensional reduction: for Burgers dynamics, only $2$-$4$ principal components capture about $95\%$ of the latent-space variance, while $4$-$7$ capture about $99\%$, depending on the initial-condition family; the same qualitative compression holds for the heat and wave equations. We also split the wavenumber axis into bands (``Fourier shells'') and measure how much each band contributes to every principal component. The resulting frequency profile is invariant under the change-of-basis freedom that the orthogonalization penalty leaves in the latent space, and is therefore reproducible across independent training runs. More broadly, this establishes the learned spectral profiles and principal components as robust observables of solution-manifold geometry.

infographic.key_insight: A physics-informed multihead neural network can learn a low-dimensional latent manifold for PDE solution families, revealing robust principal directions and frequency patterns beyond the initial condition.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This gives engineers a compact, reproducible way to summarize and compare parameterized PDE dynamics, which can support reduced-order modeling and faster analysis.
method: Shared PINN body learns a latent embedding of the solution space.
Linear heads reconstruct individual PDE solutions for different initial conditions.
A head-orthogonalization penalty removes latent degeneracies and stabilizes the basis across runs.
infographic.evidence_signal: On the 1D viscous Burgers equation, a latent size of 20 compressed to about 2–4 principal components for 95% variance and 4–7 for 99%, with similar qualitative behavior for heat and wave equations.
infographic.watch_item: The latent coordinates are not unique because orthogonalization still leaves a basis-change freedom, so interpretation should rely on the invariant spectral profiles rather than individual components alone.
infographic.paper_title: Physics-Informed Neural Embeddings of PDE Solution Families

title: Leveraging Extragradient for Effective Sharpness-Aware Minimization in Deep Learning
authors: Yao Fu, Chunxia Zhang, Junmin Liu, Yihang Jin, Haishan Ye, Yuanao Yang
abstract: Generalization remains a pivotal challenge in deep learning, where traditional optimizers like Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) often converge to sharp minima, leading to overfitting and reduced performance on unseen data. Building on Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM), for seeking flat minima associated with improved generalization, we propose the Extragradient-Inspired Sharpness-Aware Minimization (EISAM), a novel optimizer that enhances generalization via the extragradient technique. EISAM uses a two-step update process: a prediction step investigating the geometry of the loss landscape and a perturbation step that refines updates with a base optimizer. This approach achieves better generalization performance than SAM. Crucially, EISAM reduces sensitivity to the perturbation radius, enhancing robustness, and simplifying the tuning across diverse settings. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that EISAM consistently outperforms SGD, Adaptive Moment Estimation (Adam), and SAM in test accuracy and training efficiency across various architectures. Theoretical analysis further confirms that EISAM tightens the generalization bound by steering parameters toward flatter minima with reduced curvature. Accompanied by a thorough hyperparameter analysis, EISAM offers practical tuning guidance, establishing it as a robust, scalable, and broadly applicable optimization solution that advances both the theory and practice in deep learning.

infographic.key_insight: EISAM improves deep learning generalization by combining sharpness-aware minimization with an extragradient-style two-step update that seeks flatter minima more robustly than SAM.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The method offers a practical optimizer for training deep models with better test accuracy, greater robustness to perturbation-radius tuning, and strong scalability across architectures.
method: Prediction step explores the local loss geometry before updating.
Perturbation step refines the update using a base optimizer.
Extragradient-inspired design reduces sensitivity to sharpness radius settings.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports extensive benchmark experiments showing EISAM consistently outperforms SGD, Adam, and SAM in test accuracy and training efficiency across multiple architectures.
infographic.watch_item: As with other sharpness-aware methods, EISAM may add tuning and compute overhead from the two-step update, so implementation efficiency and hyperparameter settings still matter.
infographic.paper_title: Leveraging Extragradient for Effective Sharpness-Aware Minimization in Deep Learning

title: Learning Adaptive Coarse Spaces Using Transferable Neural Network Models for Linear and Nonlinear Overlapping Domain Decomposition Methods
authors: Axel Klawonn, Martin Lanser, Janine Weber-Hamacher
abstract: Domain decomposition methods have been established as efficient and parallel scalable iterative solvers and preconditioners for the solution of large-scale systems arising from the discretization of partial differential equations. In particular, overlapping Schwarz methods have been successfully applied to a wide range of linear and nonlinear problems. However, for problems with highly heterogeneous coefficients, standard domain decomposition methods typically suffer from deteriorating convergence rates. Robustness with respect to the coefficient contrast can be achieved by enriching the coarse space with adaptively selected constraints obtained from local generalized eigenvalue problems. The construction of these adaptive coarse spaces, however, can account for a significant part of the overall computing time. In the present work, machine learning techniques are employed to reduce this part of the computing time in the context of the adaptive Generalized Dryja-Smith-Widlund (AGDSW) coarse space. A two-stage approach is proposed in which regression neural networks are used to predict the adaptive coarse basis functions, while a classification neural network is employed to predict the number of basis functions required to ensure robustness. As a consequence, adaptive coarse spaces can be set up in the online phase without solving any eigenvalue problem. Particular attention is paid to problem-specific aspects, including sign-invariant loss functions and post-processing strategies to significantly improve the predicted constraints. The proposed approach is first investigated for scalar diffusion problems with high coefficient contrasts and is subsequently transferred, without retraining, to problems of linear elasticity and to nonlinear $p$-Laplace problems, also within a nonlinear Schwarz framework.

infographic.key_insight: The paper replaces expensive eigenvalue-based adaptive coarse-space construction in overlapping Schwarz methods with transferable neural networks that predict both the needed coarse constraints and their basis functions.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This can substantially reduce online setup cost while preserving robustness for high-contrast PDE solvers in linear and nonlinear large-scale simulations.
method: Train a regression network to predict adaptive coarse basis functions.
Train a classification network to predict how many basis functions are needed for robustness.
Use sign-invariant losses and post-processing, then transfer the models across diffusion, elasticity, and p-Laplace problems without retraining.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports evaluation on scalar diffusion with high coefficient contrast and transfer tests to linear elasticity and nonlinear p-Laplace problems within a nonlinear Schwarz framework.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on reliable preprocessing/post-processing and may be sensitive to how well the learned predictions generalize across PDE families and coefficient contrasts.
infographic.paper_title: Learning Adaptive Coarse Spaces Using Transferable Neural Network Models for Linear and Nonlinear Overlapping Domain Decomposition Methods

title: TREK: Distill to Explore, Reinforce to Refine
authors: Yuanda Xu, Zhengze Zhou, Kayhan Behdin, Jelena Markovic-Voronov, Hejian Sang, Xiaomin Li
abstract: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) is effective when the current policy already samples useful reasoning trajectories, but it stalls on hard prompts whose correct solution modes lie outside the student's on-policy support. We propose TREK (Teacher-Routed Exploration via Forward KL), a simple staged procedure that uses distillation not for imitation but for exploration support expansion. A key advantage of TREK is its generality: because it only consumes verified output trajectories, it can use an external black-box teacher, a white-box teacher, or the same model given additional inference-time context, and it can efficiently identify which hard-prompt samples are most worth consolidating even when teacher internals are unavailable. TREK first identifies prompts where the unaided student has very low pass rate, queries a proposal source to produce verified candidate solutions, keeps the top-$r$ proposals ranked by current student likelihood, applies a short forward-KL phase to pull those verified modes into the student's support, and then returns to standard on-policy GRPO refinement. On mathematical reasoning, TREK with DeepSeek-V4 proposals improves Qwen3 models across all tested scales on AIME 2024 and AIME 2025; for Qwen3-8B, it improves AIME 2025 from 36.9 to 40.3 and AIME 2024 from 47.9 to 51.1 (avg@16), while the self-context variant reaches 38.5 and 49.6 without an external teacher. On agentic tasks, TREK raises ALFWorld success rate from 75.8 to 82.8 and ScienceWorld success rate from 12.5 to 26.7; notably, on the hardest task types, TREK achieves high success rates early in training while unaided GRPO requires substantially more optimization steps to reach comparable levels.

infographic.key_insight: TREK expands a student model’s reasoning support with verified proposals before reinforcement learning, helping GRPO escape hard prompts whose correct solutions are initially outside the on-policy support.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The method is practical because it can use an external teacher, a white-box teacher, or the model itself with extra context, making it easy to deploy when teacher internals are unavailable.
method: Find hard prompts where the student’s pass rate is very low.
Generate verified candidate solutions from a proposal source and keep the top-r proposals by student likelihood.
Apply a short forward-KL distillation phase to add those modes to support, then resume standard on-policy GRPO refinement.
infographic.evidence_signal: In the abstract, TREK improves Qwen3-8B on AIME 2025 from 36.9 to 40.3 and AIME 2024 from 47.9 to 51.1 (avg@16), and also boosts ALFWorld and ScienceWorld success rates.
infographic.watch_item: TREK depends on the quality of verified proposals and prompt filtering, so weak proposal generation or noisy verification could limit gains or add extra pipeline complexity.
infographic.paper_title: TREK: Distill to Explore, Reinforce to Refine

title: Why Pure Reasoning is Not Enough: Nature as the Source of Mathematical Innovation
authors: Charanjit S. Jutla, Vimal Sharma
abstract: We advance the hypothesis that human mathematical reasoning, constrained by both the undecidability and the computational intractability of even modest logical fragments, relies fundamentally on pattern matching from domains external to pure deduction. The most prolific reservoir of such patterns is the natural world, whose physical laws and biological systems have undergone billions of years of ``pre-computation'' and already exhibit surprisingly innovative solutions. To ground this claim, we trace the history of the Fourier transform and relevant mathematics, from the vibrating string controversy to the hear equation and subsequent formalisms prevalent in mathematics. At each critical juncture, a physics problem forced the acceptance or creation of a mathematical tool that pure formal reasoning failed to anticipate or, worse, human reasoning had resisted. We further survey the landscape of logical complexity, from NP-hard propositional satisfiability to the non-elementary decision-procedures for monadic second-order theories, to demonstrate that even when a logic is decidable, the resources required for worst-case deduction are astronomically prohibitive. We argue that these barriers make physics-inspired pattern matching not just a historical accident but a cognitive necessity. Finally, we draw the consequence for artificial intelligence: if pure reasoning is constitutively insufficient, then any system aiming at human-level mathematical creativity must embed a vast store of cross-domain patterns rather than rely on deduction alone. This furnishes a principled justification for the enormous scale of contemporary large language models.

infographic.key_insight: The paper argues that mathematical creativity is driven not by pure deduction alone, but by pattern matching from external domains—especially physics and biology, which supply innovative structures discovered through “pre-computation” in nature.
infographic.engineering_relevance: For AI systems that aim to match human-level mathematical invention, this suggests prioritizing broad cross-domain pattern stores and retrieval over reliance on symbolic reasoning alone.
method: Trace historical cases where physics forced new math: vibrating strings, Fourier analysis, and the heat equation.
Survey logical complexity barriers such as NP-hard satisfiability and non-elementary decision procedures.
Use these examples to motivate a cognitive model of innovation based on analogical pattern transfer.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract is argument-driven and historical rather than experimental; its support comes from examples in mathematics/physics history and complexity-theoretic limits, not from new benchmark data.
infographic.watch_item: The claim that large language models succeed because of broad pattern stores is plausible but remains a conceptual argument; it does not yet establish causal evidence or quantify how much deduction versus retrieval is needed.
infographic.paper_title: Why Pure Reasoning is Not Enough: Nature as the Source of Mathematical Innovation

title: Unified convergence analysis for gradient descent optimization methods in the training of deep neural networks
authors: Shokhrukh Ibragimov, Arnulf Jentzen
abstract: Gradient based optimization methods are nowadays the methods of choice for training deep neural networks (DNNs) in artificial intelligence (AI) systems. In practically relevant DNN training problems, one does usually not apply the standard gradient descent (GD) optimization method but instead one employs suitable sophisticated GD optimization methods, which incorporate adaptivity and/or acceleration techniques, such as the famous Adam optimizer. It is a key contribution of this work to provide a general unified convergence analysis for GD optimization methods in the training of DNNs with analytic activations such as the softplus and the popular Gaussian error linear unit (GeLU) activation. Our general unified convergence result applies to a large class of gradient based optimization methods such as the standard GD, the momentum, the Nesterov accelerated gradient (NAG), the RMSprop, the Adam, the Adamax, the Nadam, the Nadamax, the Adan, the AdaBelief, the AMSGrad, and the Yogi optimizers. Our analysis employs the theory of Kurdyka-Łojasiewicz (KL) inequalities to establish convergence to critical points in the training of DNNs. To the best of our knowledge, the generality of our convergence analysis is also just in the special situation of the Adam optimizer a new contribution to the literature on the analysis of AI optimization algorithms.

infographic.key_insight: The paper proves a unified convergence framework showing that many popular gradient-based optimizers can converge to critical points when training deep neural networks with analytic activations.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This gives practitioners theoretical reassurance that widely used optimizers like Adam, AMSGrad, and NAG can be analyzed under one convergence theory for DNN training.
method: Uses Kurdyka–Łojasiewicz (KL) inequalities to prove convergence.
Covers a broad optimizer family: GD, momentum, NAG, RMSprop, Adam variants, and more.
Focuses on analytic activations such as softplus and GeLU.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports a theoretical convergence analysis rather than a new dataset benchmark, with results stated for a large class of optimizers in DNN training.
infographic.watch_item: The guarantee is convergence to critical points, not necessarily global minima, and it applies under assumptions such as analytic activations and the paper’s optimization setup.
infographic.paper_title: Unified convergence analysis for gradient descent optimization methods in the training of deep neural networks

title: PluraMath: Extending Mathematical Reasoning Evaluation Beyond High-Resource Languages
authors: Daryna Dementieva, Nikolay Babakov, Kathy Hämmerl, Ilseyar Alimova, Jindřich Libovický, Shu Okabe
abstract: Mathematical reasoning has become a central task for evaluating and tuning reasoning Large Language Models (LLMs), yet existing benchmarks remain heavily biased toward high-resource languages, with English and Chinese dominating both pre-training corpora and evaluation suites. The recently released PolyMath (Wang et al., 2025) dataset represents a significant step forward, yet its coverage is still limited to 18 only high-resource languages. To address this gap, we introduce PluraMath, an extension of PolyMath to 18 additional {underrepresented languages spanning 6 language families -- ranging from mid-resource to extreme low-resource settings. We constructed the dataset through a human-curated pipeline, where native speakers thoroughly validated pre-computed translations. Using PluraMath, we then benchmark 27 reasoning LLMs across four model scales -- small, mid-size, large, and closed-source ensembles -- probing the multilingual mathematical reasoning capabilities of state-of-the-art models under diverse linguistic conditions. Our fine-grained analysis confirms a persistent gap in mathematical reasoning performance between high-resource and underrepresented languages, with stronger results largely associated with better instruction-following ability. We fully open-source our dataset, data acquisition pipeline, and evaluation framework, with the goal of lowering the barrier to multilingual benchmark development for underrepresented communities.

infographic.key_insight: PluraMath shows that multilingual mathematical reasoning still drops sharply for underrepresented languages, even in strong modern LLMs.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The benchmark helps engineers evaluate and improve reasoning models for real-world multilingual deployment beyond English and Chinese.
method: Extended PolyMath with 18 additional underrepresented languages across 6 language families.
Used a human-curated pipeline where native speakers validated pre-computed translations.
Benchmarked 27 reasoning LLMs across small, mid-size, large, and closed-source ensemble settings.
infographic.evidence_signal: The study reports persistent performance gaps between high-resource and underrepresented languages, with better results linked to stronger instruction-following ability.
infographic.watch_item: Because the dataset relies on translated items, translation quality and language-specific ambiguity can still affect measured reasoning performance.
infographic.paper_title: PluraMath: Extending Mathematical Reasoning Evaluation Beyond High-Resource Languages

title: On the Condition Number Upper Bound of the L-BFGS Inverse Hessian Approximation Matrix with a Two-Sided Geometric Envelope Safeguarding Mechanism
authors: Don Li
abstract: The limited-memory BFGS (L-BFGS) algorithm is a cornerstone of large-scale optimization due to its linear memory and computational costs. However, in ill-conditioned or non-convex landscapes, the implicit inverse Hessian approximation can suffer from an exploding condition number, leading to numerical instability and degraded convergence. To address this, we propose Two-Sided L-BFGS, a safeguarded variant that dynamically constrains the condition number of the inverse Hessian operator via a two-sided geometric envelope. Moreover, we show that Two-Sided L-BFGS preserves accumulated curvature information and maintains standard $O(mn)$ memory and per-iteration time complexities. We prove that this geometric envelope yields a uniform bound on the condition number of every inverse Hessian approximation generated by the algorithm. By tracking the algebraic evolution of the extreme eigenvalues through $m$ consecutive quasi-Newton updates starting from a scaled identity matrix, the resulting bound is expressed explicitly as a function of the memory depth, problem dimension, and envelope hyperparameters. Moreover, we show that Two-Sided L-BFGS preserves asymptotic global convergence in non-convex regimes under standard smoothness and strong Wolfe line-search assumptions, matching the theoretical guarantees of L-BFGS variants utilizing the Li-Fukushima cautious update rule. Numerical experiments on high-dimensional optimization problems demonstrate that the proposed method maintains well-conditioned inverse Hessian approximations and improves robustness and convergence behavior on ill-conditioned benchmarks.

infographic.key_insight: Two-Sided L-BFGS adds a two-sided geometric safeguard that keeps the inverse Hessian approximation uniformly well-conditioned while preserving the efficiency of standard L-BFGS.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This improves the stability and robustness of large-scale optimization in ill-conditioned settings without changing the usual O(mn) memory and per-iteration cost.
method: Bound the inverse Hessian condition number with a geometric envelope.
Track extreme eigenvalues across m quasi-Newton updates from a scaled identity start.
Retain standard L-BFGS curvature accumulation under strong Wolfe line search assumptions.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports numerical experiments on high-dimensional optimization problems showing better-conditioned inverse Hessian approximations and improved robustness on ill-conditioned benchmarks.
infographic.watch_item: The safeguard adds envelope hyperparameters and relies on strong Wolfe line search assumptions, so practical tuning and line-search compliance may affect performance.
infographic.paper_title: On the Condition Number Upper Bound of the L-BFGS Inverse Hessian Approximation Matrix with a Two-Sided Geometric Envelope Safeguarding Mechanism

title: SplineNet: An Isogeometric Deep Learning Method for Complex Shells
authors: Shizhou Luo, Xiaodong Wei
abstract: We present a novel isogeometric deep learning method, termed SplineNet, for the seamless design and analysis of shell structures with complex geometries. The proposed approach is built upon watertight spline representations, e.g., analysis-suitable unstructured T-splines, and features exact geometric descriptions of Computer-Aided Design (CAD) models in neural networks. Bézier extraction is used to build the network architecture, where Bernstein polynomials serve as the nonlinear activation functions. SplineNet can be applied in a data-free or data-driven way. In the data-free case, energy-based formulations can be naturally incorporated as loss terms, which fulfill the need of Computer-Aided Engineering (CAE) and can be accurately calculated. In particular, the Kirchhoff--Love (KL) model is adopted to solve for the mechanical behaviors of shell structures. This way, CAD and CAE can be tightly integrated in a deep neural network without the time-consuming model/data exchange process. In the data-driven case, SplineNet can be used as the trunk net of Deep Operator Networks (DeepONet) to provide interpretability. Given such a trained network and unseen input data, results can be immediately obtained without retraining the network or repeatedly performing the traditional workflow for analysis. In the end, a variety of numerical examples are studied to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method, especially when real-world complex geometries are involved.

infographic.key_insight: SplineNet embeds exact spline-based CAD geometry directly into a neural network, enabling isogeometric analysis of complex shell structures with a single unified model.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This tight CAD-CAE integration can reduce model exchange overhead and support fast shell simulation for complex real-world geometries.
method: Uses watertight spline representations such as analysis-suitable unstructured T-splines.
Builds the network with Bézier extraction and Bernstein polynomial activations.
Supports both data-free energy-based training with Kirchhoff--Love shell mechanics and data-driven DeepONet-style inference.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports a variety of numerical examples showing the method works well, especially for real-world complex geometries.
infographic.watch_item: The approach depends on high-quality spline/CAD representations and careful implementation of isogeometric operators, which may limit ease of adoption.
infographic.paper_title: SplineNet: An Isogeometric Deep Learning Method for Complex Shells

title: LLM-as-a-Verifier: A General-Purpose Verification Framework
authors: Jacky Kwok, Shulu Li, Pranav Atreya, Yuejiang Liu, Yixing Jiang, Chelsea Finn
abstract: Scaling pre-training, post-training, and test-time compute have become the central paradigms for improving the capabilities of LLMs. In this work, we identify verification, the ability to determine the correctness of a solution, as a new scaling axis. To unlock this and demonstrate its effectiveness, we introduce LLM-as-a-Verifier, a general-purpose verification framework that provides fine-grained feedback for agentic tasks without requiring additional training. Unlike standard LM judges that prompt LLMs to produce discrete scores for candidate solutions, LLM-as-a-Verifier computes the expectation over the distribution of scoring token logits to generate continuous scores. This probabilistic formulation enables verification to scale along multiple dimensions: (1) score granularity, (2) repeated evaluation, and (3) criteria decomposition. In particular, we show that scaling the scoring granularity leads to better separation between positive and negative solutions, resulting in more calibrated comparisons. Moreover, scaling repeated evaluation and criteria decomposition consistently lead to additional gains in verification accuracy through variance and complexity reduction. We further introduce a cost-efficient ranking algorithm for selecting the best solution among candidates using the verifier's continuous scores. LLM-as-a-Verifier achieves state-of-the-art performance on Terminal-Bench V2 (86.5%), SWE-Bench Verified (78.2%), RoboRewardBench (87.4%), and MedAgentBench (73.3%). Beyond verification, the fine-grained signals from LLM-as-a-Verifier can also serve as a proxy for estimating task progress. We build an extension for Claude Code, enabling developers to monitor and improve their own agentic systems. Finally, we show that LLM-as-a-Verifier can provide dense feedback for RL, improving the sample efficiency of SAC and GRPO on robotics and mathematical reasoning benchmarks.

infographic.key_insight: Verification is treated as a new scaling axis for LLMs, and LLM-as-a-Verifier turns judgment into continuous, fine-grained scores instead of discrete labels.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This gives practitioners a drop-in way to rank agent outputs, monitor task progress, and provide denser feedback for improving agentic systems and RL training.
method: Compute the expectation over scoring-token logits to produce continuous verification scores.
Scale verification along three axes: score granularity, repeated evaluation, and criteria decomposition.
Use a cost-efficient ranking algorithm to select the best candidate from multiple solutions.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports state-of-the-art results on multiple benchmarks, including Terminal-Bench V2 (86.5%), SWE-Bench Verified (78.2%), RoboRewardBench (87.4%), and MedAgentBench (73.3%).
infographic.watch_item: Performance may depend on careful prompt design and calibration of scoring tokens, so deployment should validate robustness across tasks and model variants.
infographic.paper_title: LLM-as-a-Verifier: A General-Purpose Verification Framework

title: Automation Without Understanding
authors: Jun-Yong Park
abstract: Two developments are unfolding at once: artificial intelligence systems have begun to produce genuine research-level mathematics, and the United States is weakening the pipeline that produces humans capable of understanding what such systems are doing. This essay argues that, taken together, these developments amount to a strategic error. Mathematical capacity, which is the trained ability to verify, interpret, and challenge mathematical reasoning, is not a byproduct of theorem production but a form of infrastructure, built over generations by institutions that cannot be reconstituted on demand. Drawing on the May 2026 AI disproof of a longstanding Erdős conjecture on the planar unit distance problem and on recent disruptions to federal support for the mathematical sciences, the essay makes the case for treating mathematical capacity as a strategic asset on a par with semiconductor capability. It further proposes, among other measures, that AI systems performing consequential reasoning be required to expose their decision-critical claims in formal, machine-checkable form, converting part of AI reasoning from opaque persuasion into auditable structure.

infographic.key_insight: The paper argues that AI can generate advanced mathematics, but without enough human mathematical capacity to verify and audit it, that progress becomes strategically risky rather than fully beneficial.
infographic.engineering_relevance: For theorem-proving systems, this implies a need for formal, machine-checkable outputs and stronger human-in-the-loop validation so high-stakes reasoning remains auditable.
method: Links an AI-discovered disproof of an Erdős conjecture to the broader issue of verification capacity
Frames mathematical expertise as strategic infrastructure, not just a byproduct of theorem generation
Recommends exposing decision-critical claims in formal proof objects for auditability
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract cites a May 2026 AI disproof of a longstanding Erdős conjecture and recent cuts to federal support for mathematical sciences as motivating evidence, rather than reporting a new benchmark evaluation.
infographic.watch_item: Requiring formal, machine-checkable claims could add overhead and may still leave gaps if the underlying formalization or human oversight pipeline is underfunded.
infographic.paper_title: Automation Without Understanding

title: Groebner.jl: Fast Gröbner Tracing in Julia
authors: Alexander Demin
abstract: A standard way to control expression swell in computer algebra is to use multi-modular or evaluation-interpolation methods. In computations involving Gröbner bases, these techniques typically require repeatedly computing Gröbner bases of specializations of the same ideal. These repeated computations can be accelerated through precomputation, notably using Traverso's tracing. We present Groebner.jl (https://github.com/sumiya11/Groebner.jl), a Julia implementation of the F4 algorithm that exposes Traverso's tracing through a reusable public interface. The implementation supports SIMD-friendly coefficient types, such as tuples of machine integers, which Julia compiles to efficient code with little manual intervention. This lets other Julia software leverage tracing to obtain speedups in applications such as structural identifiability of ordinary differential equation models and polynomial system solving.

infographic.key_insight: Groebner.jl makes Gröbner-basis tracing reusable in Julia, turning repeated specialized computations into a faster workflow for modular and evaluation-interpolation methods.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The implementation exposes Traverso-style tracing through a public API and supports SIMD-friendly coefficient types, making it practical for downstream symbolic and systems-modeling software to gain speedups with minimal manual tuning.
method: Implements the F4 Gröbner-basis algorithm in Julia
Exposes Traverso’s tracing as a reusable public interface
Uses coefficient representations like tuples of machine integers that Julia can compile efficiently
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports practical speedups in applications such as structural identifiability for ODE models and polynomial system solving, but does not provide numerical benchmarks here.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on how well the traced specializations and SIMD-friendly coefficient types match the target problem, so gains may be less pronounced outside those settings.
infographic.paper_title: Groebner.jl: Fast Gröbner Tracing in Julia

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Dynamic Battery Management of Autonomous Order Pickers
authors: Taniya Shaji, Abhay Sobhanan, Christof Defryn
abstract: Battery charging of Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs) in warehouses is a critical operational challenge that heavily impacts both order processing times and throughput. In this study, we address the dynamic AMR charging problem under stochastic order arrivals, where robots must learn optimal charging decisions. Traditional fixed-rule heuristics often prove suboptimal in dynamic environments and fail to account for multi-AMR coordination, leading to severe resource inefficiencies. To overcome these limitations, we propose a Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO)-based Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) framework designed for multi-block warehouses with fixed charging stations. Our model dynamically learns two key decisions: charging station selection and optimal charging duration, explicitly accounting for anticipated queuing times at the stations. Extensive numerical experiments benchmark the proposed model against state-of-the-art DRL and traditional heuristic approaches. Results demonstrate that our PPO framework increases order-completion rates by up to 6\% compared to the strongest baseline, while significantly reducing the total time dedicated to recharging operations. Furthermore, we validate the model's robustness across diverse warehouse configurations and stochastic arrival rates. Finally, we interpret the learned DRL policy, offering valuable operational insights into its superiority over standard benchmarks.

infographic.key_insight: A PPO-based deep reinforcement learning policy learns when and where autonomous order pickers should charge, improving warehouse throughput under stochastic demand.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This can reduce queueing, cut recharging downtime, and support more efficient multi-robot warehouse operations with fixed charging stations.
method: Uses Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) for dynamic charging decisions.
Chooses both charging station and charging duration while accounting for predicted queue times.
Targets a multi-block warehouse with multiple AMRs and stochastic order arrivals.
infographic.evidence_signal: Numerical experiments across different warehouse configurations and arrival rates show up to a 6% higher order-completion rate than the strongest baseline, with lower total recharging time.
infographic.watch_item: Performance gains may depend on how accurately the environment and queueing dynamics are modeled, so real-world deployment will need careful validation and recalibration.
infographic.paper_title: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Dynamic Battery Management of Autonomous Order Pickers

title: Regime-Conditional Stabilisation of LLM-Augmented Cooperative Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
authors: Faid Keddouri, Sohaib Houhou, Aissa Boulmerka, Nadir Farhi
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a natural interface for translating human objectives into reward signals for cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), yet the training-time dynamics of this integration remain poorly understood. We show that dynamically updating LLM-generated reward weights during off-policy MARL violates the stationarity assumption of Potential-Based Reward Shaping (PBRS) and contaminates the experience replay buffer, whose stored transitions carry reward labels computed under stale shaping weights. We characterise the result as a regime-dependent failure whose severity depends on how competent the unshaped baseline already is. To control it we propose two stabilisation strategies: a Phase-Based Freeze Schedule that enforces strict stationarity within training phases, and Exponential Moving Average (EMA) smoothing that bounds per-episode weight drift. We evaluate across three cooperative environments and five random seeds with QMIX, complemented by an exploratory VDN extension, yielding a three-regime taxonomy. In the augmentative regime (Simple Spread), where the baseline is functional (74.4 %), EMA significantly improves success to 86.7 % ($+12.3$ pp, $p<0.01$) while naive dynamic updates collapse it to 15.2 %. In the essential regime (Level-Based Foraging), where the baseline is broken (0.1 %), any shaping unlocks the task (95.9 % under EMA). In the supplementary regime (SMAC 3m), where the baseline is near-saturated (98.8 %), stabilised shaping preserves performance (99.9 %) while unstabilised shaping adds variance without gain. These findings establish reward-signal stationarity as a necessary design constraint and indicate that regime placement is a practical predictor of whether dynamic LLM shaping helps or harms.

infographic.key_insight: Dynamic LLM-generated reward shaping in cooperative MARL can fail when reward weights change during off-policy replay, so stabilizing the shaping signal is crucial and its benefit depends on the baseline regime.
infographic.engineering_relevance: For multi-agent systems using LLMs as reward translators, the paper gives a practical recipe—freeze or smooth reward weights—to improve training stability without breaking experience replay.
method: Phase-based freeze schedule to keep reward shaping stationary within each training phase.
Exponential moving average (EMA) smoothing to limit per-episode drift in LLM-generated reward weights.
Regime analysis across augmentative, essential, and supplementary settings using QMIX, with an exploratory VDN extension.
infographic.evidence_signal: Across three cooperative environments and five random seeds, EMA lifted Simple Spread success from 74.4% to 86.7% and rescued Level-Based Foraging from 0.1% to 95.9%, while unstabilized updates could collapse performance to 15.2%.
infographic.watch_item: Dynamic reward shaping must respect stationarity in off-policy replay; otherwise stored transitions may become inconsistent with current reward weights and destabilize learning.
infographic.paper_title: Regime-Conditional Stabilisation of LLM-Augmented Cooperative Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning

title: Robustness Verification of an Autonomous Underwater Vehicle-based Plankton Classifier
authors: Abdelrahman Sayed Sayed, Pierre-Jean Meyer, Asgeir J. Sørensen, Mohamed Ghazel
abstract: The assessment of planktonic standing stocks and microorganism structures is critical for understanding upper ocean biological processes. Currently, autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) equipped with in-situ optical imaging and artificial intelligence (AI) methods offer a promising solution for persistent surveillance, mapping and monitoring of planktonic life. However, current AI methods often lack robustness in dynamic, unstructured environments, where environmental noise and non-biological artifacts lead to frequent misclassifications. Standard convolutional neural network (CNN) classifiers often struggle with such conditions, leading to misclassifications that require time-consuming manual validation by marine biologists. To address this issue, we propose a novel robustness verification framework for in-situ plankton classifiers based on reachability analysis. We also introduce a continuous-time neural ordinary differential equation (neural ODE) classification model leveraging the high-resolution imaging capabilities of the SilCam particle imager. In this paper, we demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework by formally verifying the robustness of the neural ODE model against environmental perturbations. We demonstrate that our verification framework acts as an automated filter providing formal guarantees of model stability against ambiguous data, thereby improving the reliability of autonomous sampling and reducing the post-processing workload.

infographic.key_insight: The paper proposes a reachability-analysis framework to formally verify that an AUV-based neural ODE plankton classifier remains robust under environmental perturbations.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This enables more reliable autonomous underwater monitoring by reducing misclassifications and the manual validation burden on marine biologists.
method: Build a continuous-time neural ODE classifier for SilCam in-situ plankton images.
Apply reachability analysis to verify robustness against perturbations and ambiguous inputs.
Use the verification step as an automated filter with formal stability guarantees.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports formal verification of the neural ODE model’s robustness against environmental perturbations, but does not provide quantitative benchmark results.
infographic.watch_item: Verification guarantees may depend on the perturbation model and the fidelity of the neural ODE to real underwater conditions.
infographic.paper_title: Robustness Verification of an Autonomous Underwater Vehicle-based Plankton Classifier

title: Constrained Flow Matching via Lagrangian Dual Flows
authors: Vince Kurtz, Alexander Davydov
abstract: Flow matching is a powerful tool for generative modeling, but emerging applications in robotics, planning, and physics require inference-time constraints on generated outputs. Such constraints are often complex and highly nonlinear. As a result, methods designed for linear constraints like image inpainting are rarely sufficient, and projection or optimization-based alternatives can be prohibitively expensive. In this paper, we introduce Lagrangian Dual Flows, a new family of constrained generation techniques based on Lagrangian dual dynamics. By simply flowing a dual co-state alongside generated samples, we can guarantee nonlinear constraint satisfaction without expensive optimization subproblems, pseudoinverses, or projection steps during the denoising process. The resulting constrained generation algorithms are simple, effective, and open new theoretical connections between flow matching and primal-dual methods in numerical optimization.

infographic.key_insight: Lagrangian Dual Flows add a dual co-state to flow matching so generated samples satisfy nonlinear constraints without projection or inner optimization loops.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This makes constrained generation practical for robotics, planning, and physics where outputs must obey complex inference-time constraints efficiently.
method: Jointly evolve the sample and a Lagrangian dual variable during denoising.
Use primal-dual dynamics to enforce nonlinear constraints directly.
Avoid expensive pseudoinverses, projections, and optimization subproblems at inference time.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports a new constrained generation method with theoretical guarantees for nonlinear constraint satisfaction and claims it is simple and effective, but it does not provide specific benchmark results here.
infographic.watch_item: Performance will likely depend on the stability and tuning of the coupled primal-dual dynamics, especially for stiff or highly nonlinear constraints.
infographic.paper_title: Constrained Flow Matching via Lagrangian Dual Flows

title: Multi-Turn On-Policy Distillation with Prefix Replay
authors: Baohao Liao, Hanze Dong, Christof Monz, Xinxing Xu, Li Dong, Furu Wei
abstract: We study on-policy distillation (OPD) for agentic tasks, where an LLM agent interacts with an environment over multiple turns and a student imitates a teacher over these multi-turn interaction histories. Fully online OPD is costly because each update requires fresh student rollouts through the environment and teacher queries at visited histories. We propose Replayed-Prefix On-Policy Distillation (ReOPD), an off-environment alternative that reuses pre-collected teacher trajectories as replayed prefixes: the student acts at selected steps, while the teacher provides dense per-step supervision without executing new environment interactions. We show that multi-turn OPD introduces a prefix trap: making histories more student-on-policy improves relevance to the student, but can query the teacher on histories where its target is unreliable. This creates a two-sided distribution shift between student occupancy and teacher reliability. ReOPD addresses this by treating multi-turn OPD as a reliability-aware prefix distribution design and implements it with a simple step-decaying sampling schedule that emphasizes early, lower-shift prefixes. Across mathematical reasoning with Python and search environments over multiple teacher and student model scales, ReOPD preserves or improves OPD-level accuracy, uses zero tool calls during student training, and is at least 4$\times$ faster per training step than OPD. ReOPD therefore turns expensive agent-environment interaction into a reusable offline resource, enabling scalable distillation across tools, tasks, and environments.

infographic.key_insight: Replayed-Prefix On-Policy Distillation (ReOPD) replaces expensive fresh student-environment rollouts with replayed teacher trajectories, while carefully choosing prefix positions to avoid unreliable teacher targets in multi-turn distillation.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It offers a practical way to train agentic LLMs for math reasoning and tool use with much lower interaction cost, zero tool calls during student training, and faster per-step updates.
method: Reuse pre-collected teacher trajectories as replayed prefixes
Let the student act at selected steps, while the teacher gives dense per-step supervision
Use a step-decaying prefix sampling schedule to emphasize early, more reliable histories
infographic.evidence_signal: Across mathematical reasoning with Python and search environments, ReOPD matched or improved OPD-level accuracy and was at least 4× faster per training step.
infographic.watch_item: The prefix-sampling policy must balance student relevance against teacher reliability; overly on-policy prefixes can create a prefix trap and degrade supervision quality.
infographic.paper_title: Multi-Turn On-Policy Distillation with Prefix Replay

title: Kernel-based Operator Learning: Error Analysis, Budget Allocation, and a Physics-Informed Extension
authors: Rüdiger Kempf
abstract: We study kernel-based operator learning in a two-stage sampling framework, where an offline kernel regression operator learns a discretized representation of the target operator from input-output pairs and an online kernel reconstruction operator recovers the output function from predicted observations. Our main theoretical contribution is an explicit budget allocation condition relating the number $N$ of training pairs, the number $n$ of input observations, and the output resolution $m$. The condition is derived from a coupled error analysis that interprets the surrogate as a reconstruction from approximate data. This yields a decomposition of the total error into reconstruction and learning contributions that can be analyzed independently. As a consequence, we obtain quantitative scaling laws describing how $N$, $n$, and $m$ must be coupled to guarantee convergence and to balance offline learning and online reconstruction errors. The resulting estimates extend previous analyses of kernel-based operator learning. We further introduce a physics-informed extension that incorporates knowledge of the underlying PDE at evaluation time. Rather than encoding constraints directly into the kernel, we augment the online reconstruction step by penalizing PDE residuals at collocation points. The method requires no retraining for new inputs. Numerical experiments illustrate the theoretical findings and demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed physics-informed reconstruction strategy.

infographic.key_insight: The paper shows that kernel-based operator learning can be analyzed as a two-stage process, yielding explicit rules for how training pairs, input samples, and output resolution must be balanced to control total error.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It provides practical scaling laws and a physics-informed reconstruction method that can improve surrogate models for PDE-driven systems without retraining for each new input.
method: Offline kernel regression learns a discretized operator from input-output training pairs.
Online kernel reconstruction recovers the output function from predicted observations.
A PDE-residual penalty is added at evaluation time to enforce physics-informed consistency.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports numerical experiments that validate the error analysis and show the physics-informed reconstruction strategy is effective.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on careful coupling of budget parameters and on choosing collocation points/residual penalties well; poor settings may weaken reconstruction accuracy.
infographic.paper_title: Kernel-based Operator Learning: Error Analysis, Budget Allocation, and a Physics-Informed Extension
